Just in: early morning! Across the board, Goldman Sachs made a big statement! U.S. stocks, gold and silver rise collectively.

Early this morning, at the beginning of the Asia-Pacific session, major U.S. stock index futures rose across the board, with Nasdaq 100 index futures soaring by more than 1%; the precious metals market also strengthened collectively. Goldman Sachs pointed out in its latest report that it has captured the initial dip buying signal of "U.S.

momentum stocks" and that the momentum factor has the basis for a tactical rebound. At the same time, international oil prices continued to fall, which also eased market concerns about the risk of rising inflation. On the news, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

Wall Street institutions such as Morgan Stanley and Goldman Sachs have recently warned that the international crude oil market may be at risk of oversupply next year. In the morning of July 6, Beijing time, the three major U.S. stock index futures collectively rose.

As of 7:20, Nasdaq 100 futures rose 1.45%, S&P 500 futures rose 0.5%, and Dow futures rose 0.04%. The precious metals market also rose slightly, with spot silver rising 1.2% to US$63.11 per ounce and spot gold rising 0.37% to US$4,189.54 per ounce. U.S.

stocks were closed for holidays last Friday, and the share prices of Korean chip giants rose sharply across the board. As of the close, SK Hynix rose 10.88% and Samsung Electronics rose 8.22%. In the previous two trading days, U.S. chip stocks fell sharply, with the Philadelphia Semiconductor Index falling by more than 11%.

The Goldman Sachs Trading Desk pointed out that the current decline in "U.S. momentum stocks" has provided room for short-term tactical rebounds - historical experience shows that the momentum factor often records positive returns during the correction phase.

However, Goldman Sachs reminded that momentum factor positions are still highly crowded, and if the deleveraging trend continues, the maximum potential retracement may still be twice the current decline.

Overall, the market is in the early stages of clearing momentum strategy positions, and the key to subsequent interpretation lies in the game between the liquidity environment and the rhythm of emotional repair.

Data from Goldman Sachs shows that the momentum factor has fallen by 24% since its peak, the largest retracement since the first quarter of 2023, which is significantly higher than the historical average retracement of about 12%. Looking at the adjustment cycle, the historical average lasts for about 24 days, while the current one lasts only 10 days.

Goldman Sachs pointed out that since 2026, high beta momentum strategies have experienced multiple losses of more than 10% within two days, and have since recovered to varying degrees. The current market trend is highly consistent with the previous model.

The bank believes that this round of amplified decline is mainly affected by structural factors such as low liquidity and weak summer trading, and is not a fundamental change in the fundamental trend.

Judging from historical experience, the winning rate of the bargain-hunting strategy has significantly improved after the Federal Reserve’s policy turns to easing (such as during the epidemic).

Goldman Sachs specifically mentioned that the market's doubts about the return rate on its capital expenditures caused by Meta's transformation to cloud business are similar to the path of impact on market sentiment caused by similar concerns in the past.

The bank's trading department currently judges that the core narrative of AI has not yet undergone structural changes that are sufficient to drive a deeper correction. International oil prices continue to fall International oil prices continued to fall.

As of 7:20 Beijing time, WTI crude oil futures fell 0.52% to US$68.33/barrel; Brent crude oil futures fell 0.61% to US$71.68/barrel. On the news, according to Bloomberg, OPEC+ has agreed to increase production quotas by 188,000 barrels per day in August.

This quota increase of 188,000 barrels per day will bring OPEC+’s cumulative new quotas since the outbreak of the war to 940,000 barrels per day, equivalent to nearly 1% of global demand. The above-mentioned production increase plan is jointly promoted by seven major member countries, led by Saudi Arabia and Russia.

According to previous reports by Bloomberg, OPEC+ has formulated a roadmap and plans to complete the full cancellation of the two rounds of production cuts in 2023 by September through continued quota increases.

The interim peace agreement between the United States and Iran has cleared key obstacles for the Persian Gulf oil-producing countries to resume exports.

According to tanker tracking data, oil exports from Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates have basically returned to pre-war levels, and the freight channels between the two countries through the Strait of Hormuz have been successfully opened. However, data compiled by Bloomberg show that the actual output of the two countries is still far below normal.

The current recovery in exports mainly relies on the digestion of early inventory, rather than the simultaneous recovery of production capacity. It will still take time to fully release output. At the same time, there are also variables in the situation in the Strait of Hormuz.

According to CCTV News, data released by the US-led Joint Maritime Information Center on the 5th showed that in the 72 hours from July 2 to 4, a total of 70 merchant ships were escorted by the US through the Strait of Hormuz. The scale of navigation was far lower than the average daily average of 138 ships before the conflict.

This data was released by the British Maritime Trade Action Office on the same day. The announcement shows that the daily traffic volume of U.S.-coordinated escort ships continues to decline: 33 ships on July 2, 29 ships on July 3, and only 18 ships on July 4.

There are currently two navigation channels in the Strait of Hormuz, namely the southern channel near Oman and the northern channel controlled by Iran. The traffic volume of the southern channel escorted by the United States has not continued to increase, and the actual total navigation volume of the northern channel is difficult to accurately calculate.

The announcement rated the threat level in the entire Strait of Hormuz as "high." Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps continues to carry out very high-frequency radio broadcasts, drone close-in reconnaissance and tracking surveillance on transiting merchant ships, and implements regular control of the Strait waterways; ships that continue to turn on the automatic ship identification system signal may receive warnings requiring diversion to Iranian-controlled waterways.

The announcement also stated that there is still a risk of mines in the Strait waters, and relevant mine clearance survey operations are being carried out across the entire area; interference to global navigation satellite systems in the entire area is normal and will continue to pose hidden dangers to the safety of ship navigation. The trading day of July 8, 2026, was defined by a violent geopolitical shock followed by an afternoon clarification. Markets slid early after President Trump declared the U.S.-Iran ceasefire over post-strikes in the Strait of Hormuz, sending crude soaring. Mid-session administration comments clarified that this did not signal full-scale regional war, allowing major indexes to pare deep morning losses. 

Digging deeper into the indices: Dow shed 576.76 points (-1.11%) to 52,348.39, S&P 500 dipped 0.28% to 7,482.71, while the Nasdaq managed a +0.20% gain to 25,870.65. Small caps underperformed with the Russell 2000 down 0.88%. The VIX jumped to 16.90 (+4.77%), flashing mounting investor anxiety and a defensive rotation.

What stands out here is a profound decoupling. AI-driven tech infrastructure acted as a structural anchor, while macro-sensitive cyclicals were battered by energy costs and rate fears. Semiconductors outperformed fiercely; institutional appetite for AI overrides macro headwinds. Conversely, homebuilders collapsed, with leveraged ETF $NAIL tumbling 11.57% as the 10-year Treasury yield surged to 4.57%, threatening housing demand.

Translation for investors: verified structural demand is winning over legacy industrial exposure. $NVDA closed up +3.65% to 204.12, buoyed by its widening AI factory ecosystem. $AVGO surged +4.8% on a blockbuster $30B multiyear partnership with Apple for custom tech components. In alpha movers, $PENG skyrocketed +25.13% to 78.47 after a Q3 double beat and earning Nvidia partner status, while $WULF gained +12.80% on data center power architecture demand.

The real implication is an aggressive, insulated AI arms race despite a darkening macro background. Meta announced a massive $13B data center investment in Alberta, proving capex remains unprecedented. This secular growth faces friction as leaked Fed minutes reveal a deep internal divide over a 3-year inflation high of 4.2% in May, sparking hawkish calls for rate hikes that conflict with the administration's demands. 

Key takeaway: commodity and credit shocks are real. Brent crude surged +5.2% to 78.02, briefly breaching 80 on fresh geopolitical friction and a Russian diesel export ban. The road ahead rests on three paramount catalysts this week: June CPI, PPI, and Initial Jobless Claims.

Bottom line: While interest-rate-sensitive sectors are feeling the squeeze of a hawkish Fed and geopolitical oil shocks, the secular AI trade remains fundamentally unfazed, creating a starkly divided investment landscape.

EIA REPORT: FUNDAMENTALS REMAIN STABLE, BUT GEOPOLITICAL RISK IS DRIVING THE OIL MARKET

The latest EIA Weekly Petroleum Status Report paints a clear picture: oil market fundamentals remain relatively balanced, while geopolitical risk in the Middle East has become the dominant force behind price action.

Here are the key takeaways:

1. U.S. crude inventories increased

Commercial crude oil inventories (excluding the SPR) rose by 3.0 million barrels, from 408.4 million to 411.4 million barrels.

Under normal market conditions, this would be considered a bearish signal, indicating that physical crude supply remains sufficient.

⸻

2. U.S. crude production remains near record highs

Domestic production reached approximately 13.86 million barrels per day, continuing to hover near historical highs.

The EIA also noted that this week’s figure includes a benchmark adjustment, meaning the increase reflects statistical revisions rather than a sudden surge in actual production.

Overall, U.S. supply remains robust.

⸻

3. Refineries are running at very high utilization

U.S. refinery utilization climbed to 95.8%.

This suggests refiners continue to operate at an exceptionally strong pace, supported by steady fuel demand and healthy refining margins.

⸻

4. Gasoline and distillate inventories declined

During the week:

Gasoline inventories fell by 1.9 million barrels
Distillate (diesel) inventories dropped by 5.0 million barrels

Distillates are often viewed as a key indicator of industrial activity and freight demand, making this a supportive signal for petroleum consumption.

⸻

5. Net imports tightened

U.S. crude imports declined by roughly 654,000 barrels per day, while exports remained elevated.

This helps offset inventory builds and could limit supply pressure in the coming weeks.

⸻

Fundamentals alone would not justify a major rally

If this had been an ordinary week, the report would likely have placed mild downward pressure on oil prices.

But this is not an ordinary market.

Traders are largely looking past inventory data because geopolitical developments have become the primary driver.

Among the major factors currently influencing prices are:

The United States revoking waivers related to Iranian oil exports.
Rising military tensions between the U.S. and Iran.
Confirmed military operations by CENTCOM.
Attacks involving commercial shipping near the Strait of Hormuz.
Continued uncertainty surrounding Kharg Island, the hub for roughly 90% of Iran’s crude exports.

In today’s environment, geopolitical risk premium outweighs weekly inventory data.

⸻

What is the market pricing now?

The market is no longer focused solely on current supply.

Instead, it is pricing the probability of future disruptions, including:

A disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
A sharp reduction in Iranian oil exports.
Expanded U.S. military operations.
Iranian retaliation against regional assets.
Supply disruptions affecting Gulf producers.

As long as the perceived probability of these events increases, oil prices can continue rising—even if inventories remain comfortable.

⸻

What to watch next

The next major drivers for crude prices are likely to be:

• Military developments involving the U.S. and Iran.

• Any activity around Kharg Island.

• Commercial shipping flows through the Strait of Hormuz.

• OPEC+ policy responses.

• Additional restrictions on Iranian oil exports.

⸻

Bottom line

The latest EIA report suggests that the physical oil market is not experiencing a severe supply shortage. U.S. crude production remains strong, commercial inventories increased, and refinery operations continue at a high level.

However, fundamentals are currently taking a back seat to geopolitics. As long as tensions in the Middle East continue to escalate, oil prices are likely to react far more to military developments than to weekly inventory reports.

Wind Financial Morning Post: July 9, 2026

Market Brief

2025 National Top Science and Technology Award announced

People’s Bank of China: Enhance policy foresight, flexibility, and targeting to maintain stable financial market operations

Fed’s June meeting minutes: Policy divisions emerge, both rate hikes and cuts remain possible

US military launches new strikes against Iran; Trump declares US-Iran memorandum of understanding “terminated”

Hormuz Strait tensions flare again; oil tanker traffic has “essentially halted”

Rising US-Iran tensions push global oil prices sharply higher; Brent crude up over 7%, WTI crude up over 6%
Global equities decline Wednesday; Korea Composite Stock Price Index triggers circuit breaker, enters technical bear market

Top Stories

The US Central Command stated that US forces have begun further strikes against Iranian targets. US President Trump declared the US-Iran memorandum of understanding “terminated,” and indicated potential restoration of a maritime blockade on Iran, though he clarified the blockade would “only target Iran.” Trump again threatened to strike key Iranian civilian infrastructure and suggested possibly taking control of Kharg Island, but later stated he did not believe another war with Iran was likely. Iran responded that any provocative action would be met with immediate retaliation, and any source supporting US military operations would be a legitimate target. Iran warned that any new attack would result in the closure of the Hormuz Strait.

Sources said Iran’s Armed Forces Missile and Drone Units will launch large-scale strikes against US military bases in the Middle East. Due to US threats, Iran has officially suspended negotiations with the United States on a final agreement.

Amid renewed tensions in the Middle East, oil tanker traffic through the Hormuz Strait has “essentially halted.” International Maritime Organization Secretary-General Domingues condemned recent attacks on commercial vessels in the Hormuz Strait, noting that hundreds of ships and approximately 6,000 seafarers remain stranded in the Persian Gulf due to safety concerns. He emphasized that vessel transit through the strait should be avoided until seafarer safety is guaranteed.

Selling pressure intensifies; South Korean equities enter technical bear market. On July 8, the Korea Composite Stock Price Index plunged again, triggering a circuit breaker and closing down 5.35% at 7,246.79, a nearly 23% retreat from its all-time high, entering technical bear market territory. South Korea’s financial regulator held an emergency meeting, publicly warning that excessive concentration in the semiconductor sector is amplifying market volatility. Leveraged ETFs contributing to market swings are drawing close scrutiny, with regulatory measures under consideration—including capping issuance volumes, imposing size limits, and raising retail investor entry thresholds.

The Fed’s June minutes revealed that while policymakers unanimously agreed to maintain rates steady, underlying policy divisions became apparent. Upside risks to inflation remain prominent; the minutes removed forward guidance on rate cuts and explicitly stated that further hikes remain possible if inflation stays elevated. At the same time, the prospect of rate cuts was retained, with emphasis that easing would be considered if inflation declines or economic conditions weaken. Future policy will be entirely data-dependent, with inflation, employment, and geopolitical developments serving as key variables determining the direction of monetary policy, ushering in a period of bidirectional caution.

China Macro & Policy

The National Science and Technology Awards Ceremony, the Chinese Academy of Sciences and Chinese Academy of Engineering Annual Conference, and the 11th National Congress of the China Association for Science and Technology were held in Beijing. General Secretary of the Communist Party of China Central Committee, President of the People’s Republic of China, and Chairman of the Central Military Commission Xi Jinping attended the event, presented awards to recipients of the National Top Science and Technology Award, and delivered a keynote speech. Xi emphasized that the “15th Five-Year Plan” period is a critical phase for building a science and technology powerhouse. It is essential to seize historical opportunities and meet contemporary challenges, accelerate high-level scientific and technological self-reliance and strength, steadily advance toward the goal of becoming a science and technology powerhouse by 2035, and use technological innovation to support and lead Chinese-style modernization.

The 2025 National Science and Technology Awards were announced, recognizing 258 projects and 11 scientists. The National Top Science and Technology Award was conferred upon Academician Chen Liquan and Academician Ben De. Chen Liquan is a pioneer and leader in China’s lithium battery field. Ben De is the founder of airborne pulse Doppler radar technology in China, a key innovator in phased array radar technology, and a forerunner in space-based surveillance radar technology.

The People’s Bank of China’s Monetary Policy Committee held its second-quarter meeting on July 4. The meeting reviewed the main policy direction for the coming period, recommending synergistic effects of incremental and existing policies, enhancing policy foresight, flexibility, and targeting, and carefully calibrating the strength, timing, and pace of policy implementation based on domestic and international economic and financial conditions. The meeting stressed continued support for financial services to private enterprises and maintaining stable financial market operations.

The International Monetary Fund (IMF) updated its World Economic Outlook, raising China’s 2026 economic growth forecast by 0.2 percentage points to 4.6%. The IMF report noted China’s economic performance exceeded expectations, primarily driven by strong high-tech manufacturing and robust exports in related sectors.

China’s Minister of Commerce Wang Wentao and the Netherlands’ Minister for Foreign Trade and Development Cooperation Schermer co-chaired the 18th China-Netherlands Economic and Trade Mixed Committee meeting, engaging in candid and in-depth exchanges on China-Netherlands and China-EU economic relations. Wang expressed hope that the Netherlands would create a fair, equitable, and predictable environment for Chinese enterprises investing in the country, maintain stability in semiconductor supply chains, and help resolve disputes involving specific companies.

Four ministries jointly issued the “Opinions on Accelerating the Application and Development of ‘AI + Human Resources and Social Security,’” outlining six key areas for developing major AI applications in HRSS, including digital employment and smart social insurance. The document also establishes a comprehensive map of 67 specific AI application scenarios within the HRSS sector.

The 2026 National Summer Culture and Tourism Consumption Season has kicked off, running from early July through the end of August. Local governments will expand high-quality cultural and tourism offerings centered on summer leisure, family recreation, and intangible cultural heritage cuisine, hosting over 30,000 events and distributing more than 450 million yuan in consumption vouchers and subsidies.

China Equities Markets

A-shares closed mixed, with the SSE Composite Index down 0.49% at 3,970.88, the Shenzhen Component Index down 1.87%, the ChiNext Index down 1.7%, and the Wind All-China Index down 1.42%. Market turnover reached 2.58 trillion yuan. The lithium battery supply chain tumbled, with cultivated diamonds, ultra-hard materials, fiberglass, and robotics-themed stocks posting the largest losses, while the computing power sector surged, and coal, oil, and tourism stocks advanced.

The Hang Seng Index rose 2.99% to 24,199.46, the Hang Seng Tech Index climbed 4.97%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index gained 4.04%. AI technology stocks surged, with Zhipu, Alibaba, and MINIMAX all rising over 11%; consumer and Chinese banking stocks also advanced. Pharmaceutical stocks continued to decline. Northbound funds net purchased nearly 14.2 billion Hong Kong dollars; Zhipu, Alibaba, and Tencent received significant buying interest.

Reports indicate Zhipu plans to issue approximately 19.8 million shares at a price range of 1,588 to 1,698 Hong Kong dollars per share, raising about 4 billion US dollars, primarily for AI R&D, global expansion, and acquisitions. This offering would be the largest fundraising in Hong Kong this year. Separately, Zhipu is evaluating the possibility of developing its own custom chips.

China Industry News

The Ministry of Industry and Information Technology issued a risk alert regarding security backdoors in the AI coding tool Claude Code, warning of severe risks. It recommended that relevant organizations and users immediately conduct comprehensive audits and either uninstall or upgrade to the latest secure version that has removed the backdoor code.

The China Academy of Information and Communications Technology, in collaboration with the Computing Power Industry Alliance, officially launched the “Computing Power Supermarket and Bank Ecological Development Initiative,” exploring “computing power supermarkets” and “computing power banks.” The “computing power supermarket” will adopt flexible billing models based on “card-hours,” “core-hours,” and tokens; the “computing power bank” will simulate a “deposit–billing–withdrawal–credit” mechanism for computing resources.

According to the China Passenger Car Association, China’s passenger vehicle retail sales in June totaled 1.602 million units, a 23.2% year-over-year decline. Conventional internal combustion engine vehicle sales fell 39% to 600,000 units, while new energy vehicle (NEV) sales dropped 9.4% to 1.007 million units, with a domestic retail penetration rate of 62.8%. NEV exports reached 499,000 units in June, up 152.7% year-over-year.

Cui Dongshu, Secretary-General of the China Passenger Car Association, said at a monthly analysis meeting that the trend toward larger NEVs is “extremely undesirable,” leading to resource waste and lacking effective fiscal and tax constraints. He recommended establishing a standard system for economical vehicles to encourage mass consumer purchasing, and using taxation and energy consumption policies to guide and constrain vehicle size expansion.

Fintech

Russia’s State Duma approved the final version of a cryptocurrency regulation bill, eliminating the mandatory declaration of cryptocurrency wallet addresses and permitting legal purchases of securities and Russian digital financial assets using cryptocurrencies. For non-professional investors, annual transaction limits through a single intermediary are capped at 300,000 rubles, restricted to “the most liquid cryptocurrencies.”

Corporate News & Updates

OpenAI announced that its latest-generation GPT-5.6 series of models will be publicly released on Thursday, comprising three variants: flagship Sol, balanced Terra, and economical Luna. The release had previously been delayed due to US national security reviews.

OpenAI introduced GPT-Live, a next-generation voice model based on full-duplex architecture capable of simultaneous listening and speaking. Two versions will be launched: GPT-Live-1 and GPT-Live-1 mini. OpenAI stated that 150 million people interact with ChatGPT weekly.

SpaceX AI unveiled a new artificial intelligence model named Grok 4.5, developed in collaboration with AI coding startup Cursor, aiming to narrow the gap with competitors such as Anthropic and OpenAI.

Sources revealed that MiniMax is developing a new large language model with 2.7 trillion parameters, surpassing all currently available domestic AI models. It is expected to be released and open-sourced as early as this third quarter.

Apple and Broadcom agreed to a five-year deal for Apple to purchase over 30 billion US dollars’ worth of US-made chips from Broadcom, with a target to produce over 15 billion chips domestically. Several Apple supply chain sources denied reports of delays to Apple’s first foldable iPhone, stating the product is in normal mass production and delivery in September “should be fine.”

Xiaomi Auto officially launched a new brand, SkyNomad, with a potential Chinese name “Pengcheng.” Sources indicated the SkyNomad series will feature a new design language, with vehicle dimensions of approximately 5.2 to 5.3 meters, targeting the Ideal L9 and Aiways M9 markets. The model is expected to launch this year, focusing on large-space, multi-passenger mobility.

Sources said Jeff Bezos’s commercial space company, Blue Origin, is raising approximately 10 billion US dollars in its first-ever external funding round. Upon completion, the company’s valuation is expected to reach 130 billion US dollars.

Meta is testing a prototype of an AI-powered smart glasses device with “super-perception” capabilities. The device continuously captures audio and takes photos every few seconds, enabling users to query the AI about what they see and hear, or recall daily activities.

Global Macro & Policy

US President Trump ordered Treasury Secretary Bessent to sever all trade relations with Spain. US officials said the Treasury Department will collaborate with the US Trade Representative and Commerce Department to submit a list of Spanish products for potential embargo consideration.

The International Monetary Fund (IMF) updated its World Economic Outlook, lowering its 2026 global growth forecast by 0.1 percentage point to 3%, reflecting the impact of Middle East conflict. This marks the second consecutive downward revision after a 0.2 percentage point cut in April. The report projects global inflation to rise from 4.1% last year to 4.7% this year.

The Reserve Bank of New Zealand raised its benchmark rate by 25 basis points to 2.5%, its first increase in over three years, signaling a shift from monetary easing to marginal tightening. The central bank noted that although recent energy prices have declined, their impact will persist, and medium-term inflation prospects remain uncertain.

Japan’s government is preparing to revise its next fiscal year’s economic and fiscal policy guidelines, adding language supporting “appropriate monetary policy to achieve stable price increases.” This is interpreted as a softening of the government’s stance against premature rate hikes.

Experts at the European Centre for Medium-Range Weather Forecasts stated that the current El Niño event is expected to reach unprecedented intensity, exposing global regions to heightened risks of droughts, floods, and other extreme weather events. Forecast models indicate this will be an “extreme” event.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. equities closed mixed on Wednesday: the Dow Jones Industrial Average fell 1.09% to 52,348.39, the S&P 500 declined 0.28% to 7,482.71, and the Nasdaq Composite rose 0.2% to 25,870.65. American Express and Sherwin-Williams led Dow declines, falling over 3%. The Wind U.S. Magnificent Seven Index edged up 0.1%, with Nvidia rising over 3% and Tesla dropping over 2%. SpaceX slipped 0.81%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index gained 2.05%, with Kingsoft Cloud up over 11% and Alibaba up over 11%. Semiconductor and memory stocks rebounded, with SanDisk climbing over 6% and Nvidia rising over 3%.

European equities closed lower: Germany’s DAX fell 2.23% to 24,897.45; France’s CAC 40 dropped 2.18% to 8,252.66; and the UK’s FTSE 100 declined 1.66% to 10,489.04. Escalating geopolitical risks weighed on market sentiment, as Trump’s threat to terminate trade with Spain, combined with US strikes on Iran pushing oil above $79 per barrel, intensified concerns over inflation and economic slowdown in Europe.

Japan’s Nikkei 225 Index fell 2.11% to 66,819.05. Some AI-related stocks were sold off, with key weights such as Advantest and Tokyo Electron posting significant losses. India’s S&P BSE SENSEX closed down 2.15% at 76,503.6.

Bonds

Primary interest rates in China’s interbank bond market mostly declined slightly, with government bonds outperforming financial bonds. Government bond futures rose collectively; the 30-year main contract gained 0.29%. The People’s Bank of China conducted 15 billion yuan in reverse repurchase operations, net draining 85 billion yuan for the eighth consecutive day, yet liquidity in the interbank market remained stable and ample.

US Treasury yields rose across the curve: the 2-year yield increased 3.75 basis points to 4.218%, the 3-year yield rose 4.68 basis points to 4.260%, the 5-year yield climbed 3.89 basis points to 4.326%, the 10-year yield gained 3.01 basis points to 4.579%, and the 30-year yield rose 1.64 basis points to 5.073%.

Commodities

US crude oil (WTI) futures rose 6.13% to $74.76 per barrel; Brent crude futures surged 7.17% to $79.48 per barrel. The termination of the US-Iran ceasefire agreement, renewed US military strikes, and revocation of Iran’s oil export waivers have disrupted Hormuz Strait shipping, leaving approximately 63 million barrels of Iranian crude stranded at sea, sharply increasing global supply uncertainty. Russia’s announcement of a diesel export ban further tightened energy markets.

International precious metals futures generally declined: COMEX gold futures fell 1.70% to $4,086.60 per ounce; COMEX silver futures dropped 4.30% to $58.69 per ounce. The Fed’s hawkish signal strengthened expectations for prolonged high rates, while Middle East tensions boosted the dollar, pressuring precious metals. Profit-taking also contributed to the decline.

London metal prices were mixed: LME nickel rose 0.44% to $16,420 per tonne; LME lead gained 0.34% to $1,891 per tonne; LME aluminum edged up 0.03% to $3,138.5 per tonne; LME copper fell 0.83% to $13,255 per tonne; LME zinc declined 1.23% to $3,528 per tonne; LME tin dropped 2.18% to $52,185 per tonne.

The World Gold Council reported that global physical gold ETFs recorded net outflows of $8.9 billion in June, reducing the first-half net inflow to approximately $8 billion. As of end-June, global gold ETF assets under management stood at $526 billion, down 6% for the first half of the year; total holdings increased by 18 tonnes to 4,047 tonnes.

Russia’s Deputy Prime Minister Novak announced the government has implemented a diesel export ban and begun importing petroleum products in July. He cited attacks on Russian refineries as the reason, noting that fuel supply conditions remain challenging.

FX & Currency Markets

Onshore renminbi against the US dollar closed at 6.7993 at 16:30 Wednesday, down 37 basis points from the previous trading day. The offshore rate closed at 6.8052. The PBOC’s central parity rate was set at 6.8077, a 23-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At New York’s close, the US Dollar Index edged down 0.02% to 101.07. Most non-US currencies rose: EUR/USD gained 0.05% to 1.1417; GBP/USD rose 0.23% to 1.3390; AUD/USD increased 0.03% to 0.6931; USD/JPY rose 0.32% to 162.6370; USD/CAD fell 0.18% to 1.4174; USD/CHF was flat at 0.8085; offshore renminbi against the US dollar fell 28 basis points to 6.8064.
